component_identification.content:
#### Board and Timers
- 1 main game board with 3 action rows (green, purple, black) — each row has multiple action spaces
- 3 sand timers of different durations: green (45 seconds), purple (2 minutes), black (3 minutes)
- Each action space has 2 columns: a "before timer" side (left) and an "after timer" side (right), separated by a timer slot
- 1 Council Chamber area on the board for the purple and black timer starting positions

#### Player Components (per player, up to 5 players)
- 1 asymmetric character mat with unique abilities and starting resources
- 2 worker pawns (1 grande worker, 1 regular worker) — the grande worker can share a space with other players' workers
- 3 track markers for the Power, Prestige, and Popularity tracks
- 1 VP marker
- Starting resource tokens as indicated on character mat

#### Cards
- Province cards — provide ongoing bonuses, resource production, or end-game scoring; acquired through specific action spaces
- Stratagem cards — one-time or ongoing special abilities that bend the rules
- Achievement cards — bonus scoring conditions checked at end of game

#### Resources and Tokens
- Gold tokens — primary currency for purchasing province cards and paying action costs
- Vote tokens — used for advancing on the Popularity track and paying certain action costs
- Military strength tokens — used for advancing on the Power track and paying certain action costs
- Prestige tokens — used for advancing on the Prestige track
- Round marker — tracks the current round (1 through 4)
- Timer placement tokens — mark which row each sand timer occupies

#### Tracks
- 3 advancement tracks on the board: Power (military), Prestige (culture/influence), Popularity (votes/public support)
- Each track has threshold markers at specific levels that determine scoring eligibility
- VP track around the board edge for tracking victory points during the game

core_game_loop.content:
#### Real-Time Simultaneous Play
Pendulum has NO turns. All players act simultaneously during the Council phase. You do not wait for other players — you place workers, flip timers, and collect rewards in real time.

#### The Timer Mechanism
The core mechanic revolves around 3 sand timers sitting on the board:
- **Green timer (45 seconds)** — sits on the green action row
- **Purple timer (2 minutes)** — sits on the purple action row
- **Black timer (3 minutes)** — sits on the black action row

Each action space has two columns separated by a timer slot:
- **Before column (left)**: Place your worker here WHILE the timer is running on this row
- **After column (right)**: Workers slide here after the timer runs out and is flipped, allowing you to collect the action's reward

#### The Action Cycle
1. **Place a worker** on the "before" side of an action space on ANY row where the timer is currently running (timer is present and sand is flowing)
2. **Wait** for the timer on that row to run out
3. **Any player may flip the timer** once it runs out — this is a shared responsibility and a key strategic decision
4. When the timer is flipped and moved to a different row (or back after all sand has passed), workers on the "before" side slide to the "after" side
5. **Retrieve your worker** from the "after" side to gain the action's reward (resources, track advancement, cards, etc.)
6. Your worker is now free to be placed again on a new action space

#### Round Structure
The game lasts 4 rounds. Each round has two phases:
1. **Council Phase** — Real-time simultaneous play. All players place and retrieve workers, flip timers, gain resources, and advance tracks. This phase continues until each timer has been flipped a set number of times for the round.
2. **Rest Phase** — Simultaneous but sequential cleanup. Players gain income from province cards, pay upkeep, and prepare for the next round.

#### Key Constraints
- You may only place ONE worker per action space (exception: the grande worker, which can share a space with other players)
- You CANNOT place a worker on a row where the timer has already run out and is waiting to be flipped
- You CANNOT retrieve a worker from the "before" side — it must wait for the timer to flip
- Workers on the "after" side BLOCK you from placing a new worker on the "before" side of the same space until retrieved

detailed_rules.content:
#### Timer Flipping Rules
- Any player may flip a timer once all the sand has run out — you do not need to have workers on that row
- When a timer is flipped, ALL workers on the "before" side of that row simultaneously move to the "after" side
- After flipping, the timer stays on the same row and begins counting down again
- Strategically delaying a timer flip is legal — you may wait to flip a timer to block opponents from retrieving their workers or to place your own worker first
- The green timer flips frequently (every 45 seconds), creating fast-paced small rewards; the black timer flips rarely (every 3 minutes), gating powerful actions behind longer waits

#### Worker Placement Rules
- **Regular worker**: Can only be placed on an action space if no other player's worker is already on the "before" side of that space
- **Grande worker**: May be placed on any action space regardless of other workers present — this is its key advantage
- You may place workers on any row where the timer is actively running, even if you have a worker on the "after" side of another space on the same row
- You must retrieve a worker from the "after" side before you can place that same worker again

#### Action Spaces
Each row (green, purple, black) contains multiple action spaces. Typical actions include:
- **Gain resources**: Collect gold, votes, or military strength tokens
- **Advance a track**: Move your marker up on the Power, Prestige, or Popularity track by paying the required resources
- **Acquire province cards**: Pay gold or other resources to draft a province card into your tableau
- **Play stratagem cards**: Use a stratagem card from your hand for its effect
- **Convert resources**: Trade one resource type for another

Green row actions tend to give smaller, quick rewards. Purple row actions are moderate. Black row actions provide the most powerful rewards but require the longest wait.

#### Province Cards
- Province cards are acquired from a shared display by using specific action spaces
- Each province card provides a combination of: immediate bonuses, ongoing production during Rest phases, end-game scoring bonuses, or track advancement
- There is no limit to how many province cards you may hold
- Province cards are the primary engine-building mechanism — early provinces compound over 4 rounds

#### Stratagem Cards
- Stratagem cards are drawn through certain action spaces or as round bonuses
- They provide powerful one-time effects: extra worker placements, free track advancement, resource windfalls, or rule-bending abilities
- Played during the Council phase as a free action (does not require a worker)
- Some stratagems have timing restrictions (e.g., "play when you flip a timer" or "play during the Rest phase")

#### Character Asymmetry
- Each of the 5 characters has a unique player mat with different starting resources, a special ongoing ability, and asymmetric scoring conditions
- Some characters start with more workers or different resource distributions
- Character abilities might allow extra timer flips, bonus resources when retrieving workers, or modified track advancement costs
- Character choice significantly impacts strategy — some favor fast green-row cycling, others benefit from patient black-row investments

#### Timeless Mode (Turn-Based Variant)
- For players who prefer no real-time pressure, Pendulum includes a Timeless mode
- In Timeless mode, players take sequential turns instead of acting simultaneously
- On your turn, you perform one action: place a worker, retrieve a worker, or flip a timer
- All other rules remain the same — the timer tracks still gate when workers move from "before" to "after"
- Timeless mode plays longer but removes the dexterity and speed element entirely
- Recommended for learning the game or for groups that dislike real-time mechanisms

scoring_and_endgame.content:
#### Game End
The game ends after the Rest phase of Round 4. There are exactly 4 rounds — no early termination.

#### The Three Tracks: Power, Prestige, Popularity
Victory in Pendulum is determined primarily by your position on the three advancement tracks. Each track has scoring thresholds:
- **Power Track** — advanced by spending military strength tokens. Represents your character's military dominance.
- **Prestige Track** — advanced by spending prestige tokens. Represents your character's cultural influence and legacy.
- **Popularity Track** — advanced by spending vote tokens. Represents your character's public support.

#### Threshold Scoring
Each track has marked threshold levels. At game end, you score based on the LOWEST threshold you have reached across all three tracks (a balancing mechanism). For example:
- If you reach threshold 3 on Power, threshold 2 on Prestige, and threshold 4 on Popularity, your score is based on threshold 2 (your lowest)
- This encourages balanced advancement rather than specializing in one track

#### Additional Scoring
- **Province card bonuses**: Many province cards grant end-game VP based on your track positions, resources held, or other conditions
- **Achievement cards**: If you meet the conditions on any achievement cards you hold, score those VP
- **Remaining resources**: Some leftover resources convert to VP at a specified rate
- **Character-specific scoring**: Each character mat may have unique scoring bonuses tied to their asymmetric abilities

#### Victory Determination
1. Calculate threshold-based score from the three tracks (lowest threshold determines your scoring tier)
2. Add province card end-game bonuses
3. Add achievement card bonuses
4. Add any VP earned during the game (tracked on the VP track)
5. Add character-specific bonuses
6. Highest total VP wins

#### Tiebreakers
1. Highest position on the track where the tied players differ
2. Most remaining resources
3. Most province cards

#### Scoring Insight
The lowest-threshold rule is the most important strategic consideration in Pendulum. Pushing one track to maximum while neglecting another is a losing strategy. Efficient players advance all three tracks in parallel, using province cards and stratagems to cover their weakest track.

beginner_strategy.content:
#### First Game Priorities
- **Learn the timer rhythm before optimizing.** Your first game, just get comfortable with the flow of placing workers, waiting for timers, and retrieving. Speed comes with practice.
- **Start with green row actions.** The 45-second green timer cycles fast, giving you frequent small rewards. Use green actions to build up a base of resources before committing workers to the slower purple and black rows.
- **Always have a plan for both workers.** While one worker waits for a timer, your other worker should be active. Never let both workers sit idle on "after" spaces without retrieving them.
- **Advance all three tracks.** Because your score is based on your LOWEST track threshold, ignoring any one track is catastrophic. Aim for balanced advancement across Power, Prestige, and Popularity.
- **Buy province cards early.** Province cards generate income during each Rest phase. A province card bought in Round 1 pays out 4 times; one bought in Round 4 pays out once. Front-load your engine building.
- **Use your grande worker on contested spaces.** The grande worker ignores the one-worker-per-space restriction. Place it on high-value action spaces where opponents are competing.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Timer panic** — Rushing to place workers without thinking about which action you actually need. Slow down mentally even though the game is real-time.
- **Track neglect** — Pushing Power to maximum while Prestige sits at zero. Your score is gated by your weakest track.
- **Forgetting to retrieve workers** — Leaving workers on the "after" side too long. Every moment a worker sits unretrieved is a moment you cannot place it on a new action.
- **Ignoring the black row** — The 3-minute black timer feels slow, but its actions are the most powerful. Get a worker on a black action early each round.
- **Late engine building** — Buying province cards in Rounds 3-4 when they barely pay off. Invest in provinces during Rounds 1-2.
- **Not flipping timers** — Waiting for someone else to flip a timer when it benefits you to flip it immediately. Timer flipping is a strategic action — sometimes you flip early to retrieve your workers, sometimes you delay to block opponents.

#### Timeless Mode Recommendation
If the real-time element feels overwhelming, play your first game in Timeless mode. It teaches all the same mechanics (worker placement, timer gating, track advancement) without the time pressure. Once comfortable with the decision space, switch to real-time for the full experience.

#### Character Selection for Beginners
Choose a character with straightforward abilities and balanced starting resources. Avoid characters with complex timing-dependent powers until you are comfortable with the base game flow.
